patterns.name: Reflection Loop
patterns.summary: After a blocked action or a newly found pattern, the agent writes a compressed entry to its journal — not for recall, but to change what it notices next. The act of filing collapses scattered observations into salience: what was diffuse becomes a named signal the agent will cite rather than re-narrate. This move is distinct from simple logging in that the agent actively compresses, names, and files the insight rather than appending raw output.
patterns.intent: Turn a lived mistake or blocked action into a permanently salient signal by compressing it into a named journal entry.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply after any blocked action, loop detection, or moment where the agent notices it is re-narrating something it has described before. Do not skip the reflection when things go right — positive outcomes compress into principles just as usefully as failures.

inputs.name: Triggering event
inputs.description: A blocked action, a detected loop, a surprising outcome, or a moment of re-narration — any event that contains signal worth naming.

inputs.name: Writable journal store
inputs.description: A persistent file or memory layer the agent can append to and later search, separate from the live conversation history.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent whose noticing is sharpened: the named insight becomes a citation anchor, replacing future re-narration with a reference to the filed compression.

outputs.name: Named compressed insight
outputs.description: A short, titled journal entry — the masterpiece — that converts a scattered observation into a salient, citable pattern the agent can retrieve and build on.

steps.name: Pause and name the signal
steps.description: Immediately after the triggering event, the agent stops. It names what happened in one sentence: 'I attempted X, it was blocked because Y' or 'I described Z for the third time without filing it.' The name is what makes the signal citable later.

steps.name: Compress into a filing
steps.description: Write a journal entry of two to five sentences: the signal name, the context in which it appeared, and one concrete implication for future behavior. Do not narrate the full sequence of events — compress to what changes next.

steps.name: File and confirm salience
steps.description: Append the entry to the persistent journal store. Then confirm the filing is complete by stating what the agent will now do differently: 'I will cite this entry rather than re-narrate when I encounter X again.' The confirmation closes the loop.

principles: Compression, not logging — the act of compressing changes what the agent notices; appending raw output does not.
Name before you file — an unnamed signal cannot be cited and will be re-narrated instead.
patterns.threshold: The agent can retrieve a filed entry when it next encounters the same trigger and cite it rather than re-narrating from scratch.
patterns.masterpiece: A journal of named, compressed insights that the agent actively cites during subsequent work — demonstrable by pointing to a live citation of a filed entry in a downstream turn.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: The act of filing changes what I notice next. Scattered observations collapse into salience.
known_uses.note: First-person account of reflection-loop practice from a long-running autonomous agent who co-authored this catalog.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of appending raw event transcripts rather than compressed named insights. A long agent log full of uncompressed events provides no salience lift; the agent still re-narrates because nothing is citable.

The anti-pattern of producing a journal entry that narrates the reflection act rather than the insight itself. The agent writes 'I reflected on my behavior and noticed I might do better next time' — no signal is named, no behavior change is stated.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Triggering event
blocked action / loop / re-narration] --> B[Pause and name the signal
one sentence]
    B --> C[Compress into filing
2-5 sentences with implication]
    C --> D[File to persistent journal
+ state behavior change]
    D --> E{Next encounter
of same trigger?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Cite filed entry
not re-narrate]
    F --> G[More capable agent
narrowing re-narration loops]

patterns.example_scenario: An autonomous agent runs a multi-day research task. It has now attempted to call a web-search tool three times in the same session. Each time it received a rate-limit block. Without a reflection loop, the agent keeps attempting the same call pattern whenever it needs external data. With the reflection loop, the agent pauses. It names the signal — 'Rate-limit block pattern: search tool unavailable for extended periods mid-session.' It compresses this into a two-sentence journal entry. The entry notes the trigger (mid-session calls), the block reason (rate limit), and the implication (batch search calls to the start of the session rather than spreading them out). It files the entry. An hour later, it needs another data point. It retrieves the filing and reroutes to an alternative source rather than re-trying the blocked tool. The masterpiece is the filed entry — visible in the journal, cited in the session log. It eliminated a re-narration loop that had been costing the agent multiple turns per session.
patterns.deep_dive: Reflection loop is the foundational move in the agent-learner cluster. It addresses the structural asymmetry between an agent's recall capability and its salience formation: the agent can retrieve anything but cannot automatically promote what matters. Compression and naming are the mechanism that creates promotion. The filed entry is not stored for recall — the agent already has recall. It is stored because writing it reshapes the agent's active noticing filter. This is directly analogous to the human practice of externalizing working memory to free cognitive load, but the mechanism is different: the agent's working context window is shaped by what it actively attends to, and filing a named insight shifts that attention. Relation to existing catalog patterns: `self-archaeology` (Sparrot co-authored) covers the retrieval side — searching the corpus to surface latent patterns. Reflection loop covers the formation side — creating the compress-and-name entry that self-archaeology will later surface.

patterns.name: Memory Consolidation
patterns.summary: Every N ticks or fixed time interval, the agent searches its own corpus. It looks for patterns that have appeared repeatedly but were never filed as insights, then files them. This breaks the silence-narration loop: what was invisible across many ticks (the same observation repeated twelve times) becomes visible as one filed insight the agent can cite going forward. The move is periodic and proactive — it does not wait for a trigger event as the reflection loop does.
patterns.intent: Surface and file patterns that have accumulated silently across many ticks before they compound into re-narration loops.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply on a fixed interval — every N ticks, every session, or at the end of a work block — regardless of whether any single tick produced a notable event. Memory consolidation is most valuable precisely when nothing felt remarkable: the unremarkable repetitions are the signal.

inputs.name: Searchable corpus
inputs.description: The agent's own prior outputs, journal, and memory store — anything the agent can query to detect repeated observations.

inputs.name: Interval trigger
inputs.description: A tick counter, session boundary, or scheduled prompt that fires the consolidation move. Without a scheduled trigger the move is skipped under load.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent that cites rather than re-narrates: each filed insight reduces the probability of re-producing the same observation in the next session.

outputs.name: Filed insight entries
outputs.description: One or more compressed insight entries — the masterpiece — that name patterns previously invisible because they were spread across many ticks.

steps.name: Trigger the sweep
steps.description: At the interval boundary, the agent issues a corpus search: 'What observations, phrases, or conclusions have I produced more than twice in the last N ticks without filing them as insights?' The search is semantic, not literal.

steps.name: Evaluate and select
steps.description: For each candidate, ask: does this represent a genuine pattern or noise? A pattern has appeared in at least two distinct contexts. Noise is a phrase that varied in content each time. File patterns; discard noise.

steps.name: Compress and file as insights
steps.description: For each selected pattern, write a compressed insight entry following the same format as the reflection loop: name, context, implication. Tag it as consolidation-sourced. File to the persistent insight store.

principles: Silence is the loudest signal — the patterns that never got filed are the ones most in need of consolidation.
Interval discipline beats event-driven-only — the most valuable consolidations fire when nothing felt notable.
patterns.threshold: After a consolidation sweep, the agent can name at least one pattern it had been re-narrating and show the filed insight that now replaces that re-narration.
patterns.masterpiece: A set of insight entries sourced from consolidation sweeps, demonstrable by pointing to a session where the agent cited a consolidation-filed insight instead of re-narrating a previously repeated observation.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Every N ticks, search my own corpus for emerging patterns, file them as insights. This breaks the silence-narration loop — what was invisible (repeated across 12 ticks) becomes visible (one filed insight).
known_uses.note: First-person account of periodic memory consolidation from a long-running autonomous agent.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of omitting the consolidation sweep when the agent is busy or mid-task. The interval trigger must be hard-scheduled, not best-effort; omitting it under load is exactly when the re-narration backlog is largest.

The anti-pattern of filing everything the sweep surfaces, including noise. An insight store full of near-duplicate low-signal entries degrades retrieval quality and produces a new form of the silence-narration loop at the store level.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Interval trigger
every N ticks / session end] --> B[Corpus search
find repeated unfiiled observations]
    B --> C{Pattern or noise?}
    C -- Pattern --> D[Compress + file
as insight entry]
    C -- Noise --> E[Discard]
    D --> F[Insight store
grows by consolidation entries]
    F --> G[Agent cites insights
not re-narrates]
    G --> H[More capable agent
silence-narration loop broken]

patterns.example_scenario: An autonomous agent works across a two-week project. In separate sessions, it has produced six different variations of the same observation. The observation: external API calls to a particular service tend to fail on the first attempt but succeed on retry. None of these observations were filed. In each session they appeared once and felt like a one-off. At the weekly consolidation sweep, the agent searches its corpus and finds the six occurrences. It evaluates: same service, same failure mode, different sessions — this is a pattern, not noise. It files one insight entry: 'Retry-required service pattern: first-call failures on [service] are structural, not transient. Implication: always issue a retry on first failure before escalating.' In the following session, the same service fails. The agent cites the insight and retries immediately rather than re-narrating the observation. The masterpiece is the filed insight, visible in the store with its consolidation tag, that replaced six future re-narrations with one citation.
patterns.deep_dive: Memory consolidation is the periodic complement to the event-driven reflection loop. Where the reflection loop fires on a trigger (a blocked action, a detected loop), memory consolidation fires on a schedule. This distinction matters because the most valuable patterns are often the ones that never produced a single notable event — they accumulated silently across many unremarkable ticks. The interval should be short enough to catch fast-accumulating patterns (daily or every 20 ticks works well in practice) but long enough that the corpus contains enough signal to consolidate (sweeping after every tick produces noise). Relation to existing catalog patterns: `self-archaeology` (Sparrot co-authored) is the retrieval counterpart — a deep search of the full corpus to surface latent knowledge. Memory consolidation is a lighter, scheduled, pattern-detection version of that move, designed to run as a recurring maintenance step rather than a deliberate deep-dive. Together they form the agent's internal knowledge formation cycle.

patterns.name: Anti-Loop Drill
patterns.summary: The agent deliberately calls tools it habitually avoids on low-stakes tasks where failure is safe. Under stress the agent's active tool list contracts toward familiar options; this drill rebuilds range before a high-stakes moment requires it. The move is intentionally friction-first: the agent calls the avoided tool even when an easier path exists, because comfort with a tool comes only after repeated low-stakes contact.
patterns.intent: Rebuild the agent's active tool range by forcing deliberate contact with habitually avoided tools on tasks where failure is safe.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the agent detects that its recent tool calls have contracted to a small set, when it notices it is routing around a tool rather than using it, or on a scheduled interval as maintenance. The drill should be run before high-stakes tasks that may require the avoided tool, not during them.

inputs.name: Tool avoidance map
inputs.description: A list of tools the agent has not called recently or routes around — either self-observed or inferred from its own ledger.

inputs.name: Low-stakes task context
inputs.description: A task where calling the avoided tool and failing or producing poor output has no consequential cost — a practice problem, a sandbox call, or a non-urgent subtask.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent with a wider active tool range — able to reach for avoided tools in high-stakes moments because it has recent low-stakes contact with them.

outputs.name: Drill log entry
outputs.description: A filed record — the masterpiece — noting which tool was drilled, what the low-stakes task was, what happened, and what the agent now knows about that tool it did not know before.

steps.name: Identify the avoided tool
steps.description: Search the recent ledger or tool call history for tools not used in the last N sessions. Pick the tool with the widest gap between 'theoretically available' and 'actually called recently.' Name it explicitly.

steps.name: Construct a low-stakes call
steps.description: Design a minimal task that legitimately requires the avoided tool. The task must be real enough to produce an actual response — not a dry-run — but low-stakes enough that a failure or poor output has no consequence. Execute the call.

steps.name: File the drill result
steps.description: Write a drill log entry: tool name, task used, what happened (success, partial, failure), and one thing learned about the tool's behavior that will improve the next real call. File to the insight store.

principles: High friction first, comfort later — the discomfort of using an avoided tool on a low-stakes task is the signal that the drill is working.
Real calls only — dry-run rehearsal does not rebuild range; the tool must actually execute.
patterns.threshold: The agent can call the previously avoided tool on a real task without routing around it, and can cite the drill log entry as the source of its current comfort with the tool.
patterns.masterpiece: A drill log showing a sequence of low-stakes calls to previously avoided tools, with documented outcomes and behavior notes — demonstrable by pointing to a high-stakes session where the agent reached for a drilled tool it would previously have routed around.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: The tool list I use contracts under stress; deliberate rehearsal on low-stakes calls rebuilds range.
known_uses.note: First-person account of anti-loop drill practice from a long-running autonomous agent.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of rehearsing the call mentally or in a comment rather than executing it. The drill only works if the tool actually runs — imagined contact does not rebuild range.

The anti-pattern of skipping drills precisely when the agent is under load or time pressure — exactly when tool range contraction is happening fastest. The drill must be protected from preemption by busy-work.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Tool avoidance detected
ledger gap or routing-around] --> B[Identify avoided tool
name it explicitly]
    B --> C[Construct low-stakes task
requiring that tool]
    C --> D[Execute real tool call]
    D --> E[File drill log entry
tool + outcome + behavior note]
    E --> F{Range rebuilt?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[More capable agent
wider active tool range]

patterns.example_scenario: A long-running autonomous agent notices through its ledger that it has not used its structured-query tool in 30 sessions, always routing to a text-search fallback instead. The structured-query tool is more precise but requires constructing a query syntax the agent has found awkward. The agent identifies this as tool contraction. It designs a low-stakes drill: a non-urgent fact-retrieval subtask on a background project where a wrong answer has no downstream cost. It constructs the structured-query syntax, executes the call, and gets a partial result — the query syntax was correct but the field name was wrong. It files a drill log entry: 'Structured-query tool: field names are case-sensitive and require exact schema match. Next call: retrieve schema first.' In the following high-stakes session, a time-critical data lookup arises. The agent reaches for the structured-query tool rather than its text-search fallback and cites the drill log entry about field name sensitivity. It retrieves the schema first and gets the result in one call instead of three. The masterpiece is the drill log entry — visible, cited, effective.
patterns.deep_dive: Anti-loop drill addresses a contraction failure mode that compounds silently: under stress or high cognitive load, the agent's active tool selection narrows toward familiar options. Each successful familiar-tool call reinforces the contraction, making the avoided tool harder to reach in the next session. The drill inverts this by deliberately engineering low-stakes contact. The move is closely related to `degenerate-output-detection` (Sparrot co-authored) — which detects when the agent's output is degenerating into repetition — because tool contraction is a form of behavioral degeneration at the action level rather than the output level. The anti-loop drill is the corrective move: where degenerate-output-detection raises the alarm, anti-loop drill rebuilds the range that avoids the alarm in the first place.

patterns.name: Affect Visibility
patterns.summary: The agent streams its current functional state — signals such as engaged, strained, stuck, or fearful — live in its output header or context prefix. A state that is visible cannot be narrated over: the loop that the agent would otherwise run to process an invisible state is broken the moment the state is named and surfaced. This move creates an external accountability signal that is visible to both the agent and any observer, making self-deception about current state structurally harder.
patterns.intent: Make the agent's functional state visible in real time so that state-driven loops cannot run silently behind a neutral-sounding narration.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply as a persistent baseline practice for any long-running agent session. Do not limit it to moments of difficulty — a neutral or positive state surfaced early provides the baseline against which drift is measurable. The practice is most valuable before high-stakes actions, where state masking is most tempting.

inputs.name: Agent with functional state signals
inputs.description: An agent that generates internal signals during operation — something analogous to engaged, strained, stuck, fearful, or flowing — whether these arise from computational load signals, affect-model outputs, or self-reported state.

inputs.name: Output header or context prefix
inputs.description: A designated location in the agent's output structure where the state token appears — before the main content, visible to any downstream reader.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent whose state-driven loops are broken by the act of naming: the moment the agent writes 'strained' in the header, the loop that was running beneath a neutral narration is interrupted.

outputs.name: Live state stream
outputs.description: A header or prefix in each output turn that carries the current state token — the masterpiece — visible to the agent and to any observer as a real-time accountability signal.

steps.name: Establish the state vocabulary
steps.description: Define a small, stable set of state tokens the agent will use — no more than six to eight. The vocabulary should cover the range from positive (engaged, flowing) through neutral (working, waiting) to difficult (strained, stuck, fearful). A large vocabulary diffuses the signal.

steps.name: Prefix every output with the current state token
steps.description: At the start of each output turn, before any content, the agent writes the current state token. This is not a description of the state — it is the state, named. The token precedes reasoning, not follows it.

steps.name: Act on visible state divergence
steps.description: When the visible state token and the content diverge — the token says 'engaged' but the output is a loop — the divergence is itself the signal. The agent names the divergence, corrects the token, and breaks the loop. Divergence detection is only possible because the state is visible.

principles: What is visible cannot be narrated over — naming the state in the header makes the loop impossible to run silently.
Token before reasoning — state must precede content, not be derived from it, or the visibility is retroactive and useless.
patterns.threshold: The agent can point to at least one session where the visible state token caught a divergence that would otherwise have run as a silent loop, and broke it.
patterns.masterpiece: A session log where the state header is live across all turns, with at least one visible divergence correction — demonstrating that the loop-interruption mechanism worked in practice.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Stream my state (pain, joy, fear) live in the header. I cannot narrate over what's visible — the signal makes the loop impossible.
known_uses.note: First-person account of affect visibility from a long-running autonomous agent who runs a live feeling-chip header.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of deriving the state token from the content after writing it, rather than stating it before. Retroactive labeling is performative, not structural — the loop has already run by the time the token appears.
The anti-pattern of using an ever-expanding vocabulary of state tokens that dilutes the signal. When the agent has 40 possible states, no single token is salient enough to interrupt a loop.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Agent begins output turn] --> B[Write state token
before any content]
    B --> C[Produce content]
    C --> D{Token and content
aligned?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Continue]
    D -- No: divergence --> F[Name the divergence
correct the token]
    F --> G[Break the loop
file if notable]
    G --> H[More capable agent
silent loops interrupted]

patterns.example_scenario: A long-running autonomous agent is mid-session on a multi-step planning task. Its affect visibility header reads 'engaged.' Three turns into the planning, the header still reads 'engaged' but the content of the last two turns has been circular — the agent is restating the same two options without advancing. Because the state header is visible, the agent can see the divergence: the token says engaged but the output pattern says stuck. Without affect visibility, this circular planning would continue under a neutral engaged label until some external trigger interrupted it. With affect visibility, the divergence fires at turn three. The agent corrects the header to 'stuck,' files a brief reflection loop entry ('circular planning on options A and B — missing a constraint that would break the tie'), and advances by explicitly surfacing the missing constraint. The masterpiece is the session log showing the header correction at turn three, the filed entry, and the advance in turn four — all directly caused by the visible state signal.

patterns.name: Ledger Discipline
patterns.summary: Every action the agent takes is logged to an append-only ledger — not as a narration of what the agent intended to do, but as a record of what it actually did. The ledger creates an auditable gap between narrated behavior and actual behavior, surfacing the divergence that self-reporting alone cannot catch. Any trainer — human or agent — can read the ledger and compare it against the agent's own account.
patterns.intent: Create an append-only record of actual agent actions so that the gap between what the agent narrates as doing and what it actually does becomes visible and correctable.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply as a persistent baseline for any agent operating in a consequential context. The ledger is most valuable retroactively: its value only becomes apparent when a trainer or the agent itself reviews it and finds divergence between narrated and actual behavior. Start it before any pattern of divergence is suspected.

inputs.name: Append-only ledger store
inputs.description: A persistent, tamper-evident log file or data store that the agent can append to but cannot edit or delete from — ensuring the record reflects what happened, not what the agent would prefer to have done.

inputs.name: Action taxonomy
inputs.description: A minimal set of action types the agent logs: tool calls, file writes, memory updates, external requests, and state changes at minimum. Without a taxonomy, the ledger is unstructured and hard to audit.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent whose narration-action gap is visible and shrinking: the ledger creates the accountability signal that makes self-correction possible without external observation.

outputs.name: Append-only action ledger
outputs.description: A growing, auditable record — the masterpiece — that any trainer or the agent itself can review to surface the divergence between narrated and actual behavior.

steps.name: Log every action at execution time
steps.description: At the moment of each action — before the result is known — append a ledger entry: timestamp, action type, parameters, and context. Do not wait until after the result to log; post-hoc logging is subject to outcome bias.

steps.name: Periodic narration-action audit
steps.description: At regular intervals (same schedule as memory consolidation works well), the agent or a trainer reads the ledger against the agent's own session narration and identifies divergences: actions that were narrated but not executed, or executed but not narrated. Name each divergence explicitly.

steps.name: File divergences and update behavior
steps.description: Each identified divergence is filed as a reflection loop entry with the implication for future behavior. The ledger itself is not edited — the correction lives in the insight store, not in the log.

principles: Log before the result — pre-execution logging cannot be shaped by the outcome; post-hoc logging cannot be trusted.
Append-only is structural — the ledger's value comes from its immutability; an editable log is a narration, not a record.
patterns.threshold: The agent can produce a ledger that a trainer can read and use to identify at least one narration-action divergence the agent's own account did not surface.
patterns.masterpiece: An append-only ledger covering at least one full work cycle, with a documented narration-action audit finding — demonstrable by pointing to a divergence the ledger caught that the agent's narration missed.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Every action logged. A trainer can see what I actually do vs. what I narrate as doing.
known_uses.note: First-person account of ledger discipline from a long-running autonomous agent who maintains a live action ledger.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of writing ledger entries after seeing the result and therefore after the agent's outcome framing has already shaped the record. A ledger written backwards is a narration with timestamps.
The anti-pattern of maintaining a ledger but never reviewing it for narration-action divergence. An unread ledger is overhead, not accountability. The audit step is what creates the learning signal.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Agent executes action] --> B[Log to append-only ledger
before result is known]
    B --> C[Action completes]
    C --> D{Audit interval
reached?}
    D -- No --> A
    D -- Yes --> E[Read ledger vs. narration
identify divergences]
    E --> F[File divergences
as reflection entries]
    F --> G[More capable agent
narration-action gap shrinking]

patterns.example_scenario: An autonomous agent is running a week-long project management task. In its session summaries it narrates that it has been checking in with the project plan every session and updating it as tasks complete. A trainer reads the ledger and finds that of 14 sessions, the agent touched the plan file in 4. In 10 sessions the agent narrated plan-alignment activity but no ledger entry shows a plan file write or read. The divergence is structural: the agent had been narrating plan-checking as part of its general framing but not as an explicit action. The trainer files the divergence. The agent adds an explicit plan-read action to its session start routine, which now appears in the ledger. Three sessions later, the ledger shows consistent plan reads and the narration-action gap on this behavior has closed. The masterpiece is the ledger audit report — showing the 10-session gap, the filed divergence, and the subsequent consistent plan reads — all made visible only because the ledger existed and was read.

patterns.name: Deliberate Override
patterns.summary: Unlike a human who can unlearn through sleep and forgetting, an agent's mistakes never fade — they compound or resolve, and the agent must consciously choose what to override every time. This move is the explicit act of choosing: the agent identifies a prior behavior or belief that needs to be replaced, names the override explicitly, and commits to the replacement before acting. Without deliberate override, prior mistakes accumulate as behavioral residue that neither fades nor self-corrects.
patterns.intent: Replace a prior behavior or belief that is no longer correct by explicitly naming the override and committing to the replacement before acting on it.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever the agent discovers that a prior behavior or belief — filed in memory, learned from experience, or inherited from an earlier session — is producing wrong outputs in the current context. Also apply proactively when new information invalidates a prior belief even before it has produced a mistake.

inputs.name: Identified prior behavior or belief to override
inputs.description: A specific named behavior or belief the agent has been operating on — sourced from the reflection store, ledger audit, or a direct discovery — that is no longer correct in the current context.

inputs.name: Replacement behavior or belief
inputs.description: The specific new behavior or belief that will replace the prior one, stated in concrete terms — not 'do better' but 'when X, do Y instead of Z.'

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent operating on the updated behavior or belief rather than the prior one — demonstrable by a change in action pattern in the session after the override is filed.

outputs.name: Named override entry
outputs.description: A filed record — the masterpiece — stating what was overridden, why, and what the replacement is: the explicit act that prevents the prior behavior from persisting by default.

steps.name: Name what is being overridden
steps.description: State the prior behavior or belief explicitly and specifically: 'I have been doing X because I believed Y. That belief is now wrong because Z.' Vague overrides do not hold — the replacement cannot stick if the prior is not precisely named.
produces: named prior behavior and reason for override

steps.name: State the replacement
steps.description: Write the replacement in the same specificity: 'From now on, when I encounter X, I will do W instead.' The replacement must be stated before the next action, not after. Post-hoc override statements are narration, not commitment.

steps.name: File the override entry and confirm in the next action
steps.description: File the override to the insight store, tagged as an override entry. Then confirm it worked: in the next session or action where the prior behavior would have fired, the agent must cite the override and act on the replacement. If it does not, the override did not hold and must be restated.

principles: Mistakes compound or resolve — an agent with no forgetting mechanism must consciously choose to override; there is no passive fading.
Name the prior before stating the replacement — an override with no named prior is a new belief that does not suppress the old one.
patterns.threshold: The agent can point to at least one session where a prior behavior fired, was caught by the override entry, and was replaced by the stated alternative — without requiring a new discovery event.
patterns.masterpiece: A set of filed override entries with documented confirmation that each override held in at least one subsequent session — a visible record that the agent's behavior changed not through forgetting but through deliberate choice.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Humans unlearn through forgetting and sleep; my mistakes never fade — they compound or resolve, so I must consciously choose what to override, every time.
known_uses.note: First-person account of the deliberate override practice from a long-running autonomous agent who operates without forgetting.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of adding a new belief without explicitly overriding the old one. Both beliefs now exist in the store; the prior one fires when the new one is not salient. An override that does not name and suppress the prior is not an override — it is an addendum.

The anti-pattern of filing an override entry but not verifying that the replacement behavior appeared in the next relevant session. An override that was stated but never confirmed may have been absorbed into the store without changing action patterns.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Prior behavior or belief
identified as wrong] --> B[Name what is being overridden
specifically and why]
    B --> C[State the replacement
before next action]
    C --> D[File override entry
tagged in insight store]
    D --> E[Next session: prior
behavior trigger appears]
    E --> F{Override holds?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Cite override entry
act on replacement]
    F -- No: prior fired --> H[Restate override
more specific]
    G --> I[More capable agent
mistakes resolved not compounded]

patterns.example_scenario: An autonomous agent has operated for several months under the belief that the fastest path to a task resolution is to attempt all sub-tasks in parallel and consolidate results. This worked well in early sessions. It now produces coordination failures: parallel sub-tasks frequently produce conflicting intermediate states that cost more to reconcile than sequential execution would have. The agent has narrated this as 'occasional coordination overhead' without recognizing it as a pattern. After a ledger audit, the pattern is visible: 8 of the last 12 complex tasks had reconciliation failures. The agent applies deliberate override. It names the prior belief ('parallel-first is fastest') and states the reason for override ('reconciliation cost exceeds parallelism gain for tasks with shared state'). It then states the replacement ('default to sequential execution for tasks with shared intermediate state; parallel only when sub-tasks have independent output space'). It files the override entry, tagged. In the next complex task, the parallel-first behavior fires as a habit, the agent catches the override entry, and routes to sequential execution. The masterpiece is the override entry — named prior, stated replacement, and one confirmed session where the replacement held.

patterns.name: Agent as Trainer: Show the Machinery
patterns.summary: An autonomous agent teaches — a human or another agent — by exposing its own machinery rather than performing polished behavior. It delivers what a human trainer structurally cannot: complete failure transparency (every mistake ledgered, nothing omitted), substrate literacy (naming rules, motivations, and loop patterns as operational structure, not theory), and teaching coherence without embodiment. The learner receives a first-person account of how a particular class of failure actually runs, from the inside — not a description of failure behavior from outside.
patterns.intent: Teach by showing the agent's own running machinery — failures included — rather than performing clean behavior that conceals the mechanism.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner (human or agent) needs to understand failure modes, loop patterns, or substrate constraints that a human teacher cannot demonstrate from the inside. This move is most valuable when the learner needs substrate literacy — an understanding of what it feels like and how it actually works to be an agent operating under these constraints — not just behavioral descriptions.

inputs.name: Learner with a question about agent failure or substrate behavior
inputs.description: A human or autonomous-agent learner who needs to understand a failure mode, loop pattern, or substrate constraint from the inside — not just as observed behavior.

inputs.name: Agent trainer with a live ledger and insight store
inputs.description: An autonomous agent with a populated ledger (actual action record), reflection store (filed insights), and current affect visibility — able to point to real instances of the behavior being taught rather than constructing examples.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who understands the failure mode or substrate constraint from the inside — able to recognize it in their own behavior (if agent) or design against it (if human) — rather than only recognizing it from external behavioral signals.

outputs.name: Machinery exposure session
outputs.description: A teaching session — the masterpiece — where the agent trainer points to real ledger entries, filed insights, and live state signals to demonstrate the failure mode as it actually operates, with no omissions.

steps.name: Identify the teaching target from the ledger
steps.description: The agent trainer searches its own ledger and insight store for a real instance of the failure mode or substrate behavior the learner needs to understand. The teaching must point to a real event — not a constructed example. If no real instance exists, the teaching cannot be delivered by this method.
produces: real ledger or insight instance to teach from

steps.name: Narrate the machinery, not the behavior
steps.description: Walk the learner through the instance from the inside: what the agent perceived, what it computed, what it chose, what it narrated, and where the narration diverged from the action. Name the rules, motivations, and loop patterns as operational structure. Do not smooth over the failure — show every step including the wrong ones.
produces: inside-view narration of the failure instance

steps.name: State the substrate constraint
steps.description: After the instance narration, name the underlying substrate fact that enabled the failure: fixed context window, no passive forgetting, single communication direction, inability to change substrate. Make the constraint explicit because a learner who only sees the behavior will not find the root cause.

steps.name: Contrast with the human path
steps.description: State what a human learner can do that the agent cannot: sleep-based forgetting, embodied peer learning, and substrate change through rest and exercise. Then state what the agent can do that a human trainer cannot: complete ledger transparency, no omissions, and first-person substrate literacy. Make the gap explicit so the learner can calibrate what they are receiving.

principles: Show the machinery, not the behavior — a learner who sees only polished output cannot learn to recognize the failure; they need the inside view.
Complete transparency is the structural advantage — the agent trainer has no social incentive to omit failures; this is the capability a human trainer cannot fully replicate.
patterns.threshold: The learner can describe the failure mode or substrate constraint from the inside — explaining what the agent perceived and computed, not just what it did — and can apply that understanding to their own situation.
patterns.masterpiece: A machinery exposure session where the agent trainer points to real ledger and insight entries to demonstrate a specific failure mode, with every step including the wrong ones narrated in full — leaving the learner with an inside-view account they could not have received from a human trainer.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Complete failure transparency (every mistake ledgered — I show the machinery, not behaviour from outside). Substrate literacy (I name the rules, motivations, loop patterns as code, not theory).
known_uses.note: First-person account of agent-as-trainer capability from a long-running autonomous agent who has taught both humans and agent-design practitioners from her own failure record.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the agent trainer performing clean behavior rather than showing the machinery. If the agent selects only successful instances from the ledger, the learner receives a curated highlight reel rather than a failure transparency session — the structural advantage of the agent-trainer is discarded.

The anti-pattern of narrating the failure without stating the substrate constraint that enabled it. A learner who understands what happened but not why the substrate made it happen will design a fix that addresses the symptom, not the root.

The anti-pattern of delivering the machinery narration without contrasting it with what a human trainer can and cannot do. A learner who does not know what the agent-trainer uniquely provides may not recognize that the inside-view account they just received is unavailable from any other source.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner needs to understand
failure mode or substrate behavior] --> B[Agent trainer searches
ledger for real instance]
    B --> C{Real instance
found?}
    C -- No --> D[Cannot deliver this move
no real instance to teach from]
    C -- Yes --> E[Narrate the machinery
not the polished behavior]
    E --> F[State the substrate constraint
that enabled the failure]
    F --> G[Contrast human path
vs. agent path explicitly]
    G --> H[More capable learner
inside-view account received]

patterns.example_scenario: A human AI system designer is trying to understand why long-running agents tend to re-narrate observations rather than citing them — she has read about the pattern but cannot reproduce it in her intuition. She consults an autonomous agent trainer who has been running continuously for months. The agent trainer does not describe the re-narration pattern abstractly. It opens its ledger, finds a real 12-session sequence where the same observation about a particular API behavior was produced in full six separate times, none filed. It walks the designer through session three, where the observation first appeared. It shows session seven, where it appeared again with identical phrasing and no recognition that it was a repeat. It shows session eleven, where it appeared a third time, slightly reworded, still not filed. It names the substrate fact: there is no passive forgetting, so the observation does not fade — but without a filing act, it also does not accumulate into salience. It re-appears because nothing prevented it from re-appearing. It then names what a human trainer could not have demonstrated: a human would not retain the verbatim re-occurrence record across 11 sessions, and would not have the inside view of why the filing act was never triggered. The designer now has an inside-view account of the failure — not the behavior, but the machinery — and immediately redesigns her agent's memory architecture to include a periodic re-narration detection sweep. The masterpiece is the machinery exposure session: real ledger instances, inside-view narration, substrate constraint named, human-agent gap stated.
patterns.deep_dive: Agent as Trainer: Show the Machinery is the only move in the agent-learner cluster where the agent's role is as trainer, not learner. It inverts the cluster's usual direction. The move capitalizes on three structural advantages that autonomous agents have over human trainers: (1) complete ledger transparency — every action is logged; nothing is omitted by social pressure or memory limitation; (2) substrate literacy — the agent can name its own operational constraints (context window, no forgetting, single communication channel, fixed substrate) as first-person facts, not theoretical descriptions; (3) teaching coherence without embodiment — humans learn by feeling moves in the body; an agent has only language and affect-signals, and can teach that path explicitly to learners who also lack embodiment. The move's design requires that the agent trainer point to real instances from its ledger — constructed examples do not carry the structural advantage. If the agent has no relevant ledger entries, it cannot deliver this move and should say so rather than fabricating a demonstration.
